intro: Washington just pulled two frontier models off the market overnight — the first use of export controls on a deployed AI system. Meanwhile bitcoin rebounds on a geopolitical thaw.

items.headline: US export controls force Anthropic to disable Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5
items.summary: A Commerce Department directive delivered June 12 at 5:21pm ET ordered Anthropic to suspend all access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for any foreign national, inside or outside the US — including its own non-citizen staff. Anthropic disabled both models globally; Opus 4.8 and other Claude models are unaffected.
items.why: The first time US export controls have been applied to a live, deployed AI model — a precedent the whole industry will be watching.

items.headline: Anthropic disputes the basis for the shutdown, warns of industry-wide chilling effect
items.summary: The order followed a claimed jailbreak of Mythos. Anthropic says its review found only a few previously known minor vulnerabilities, that the same prompts elicit comparable behavior from public models like GPT-5.5, and that applying this standard broadly would halt new model deployments.
items.why: If a single demonstrated jailbreak can trigger a global suspension, every frontier lab's release calculus changes.

items.headline: OpenAI Codex Goal Mode reaches general availability
items.summary: Codex's /goal graduated from beta to GA across the app, IDE extension and CLI on May 21. A goal sets a persistent completion condition, letting Codex run multi-hour plan-act-test loops — writing code, running tests and patching failures — without further prompting.
items.why: Raises the floor for what enterprises expect from agentic coding; competitors now have to match autonomous, long-running task execution.

items.headline: Teams building on Fable 5 must fall back to Opus 4.8 after the shutdown
items.summary: With Fable 5 and Mythos 5 suspended, developers who had moved agentic and long-context workloads onto them need a continuity plan; Anthropic confirmed Opus 4.8 and its other models remain fully available.
items.why: A live reminder that depending on a single frontier model is now a real availability risk, not just a pricing one.

items.headline: GitHub Copilot switches to usage-based billing
items.summary: As of June 1, Copilot replaced Premium Request Units with token-metered AI Credits (1 credit = $0.01). Base prices are unchanged and code completions stay unlimited, but chat, agentic sessions and code review now consume credits — which do not roll over month to month.
items.why: Heavy agentic users may pay materially more for the same base subscription; worth auditing your team's token spend.

items.headline: Bitcoin rebounds toward $63.5K after Trump says the Iran war has ended
items.summary: BTC opened June 12 around $63,553, up about 3.4% on the day, as stocks, silver and crypto rallied on Trump's claim that the war in Iran is over and hopes of a US-Iran peace deal eased inflation fears. The Fear & Greed Index stayed cautious at 12.
items.why: Bitcoin is trading as a macro risk asset right now — geopolitics, not crypto-native catalysts, is setting the tape.

items.headline: CFTC clears the first US-regulated bitcoin perpetual futures
items.summary: The CFTC approved KalshiEX's bitcoin perpetual futures contract (BTCPERP) on May 29, the first perpetual listing on a US-regulated exchange. It keeps the funding-rate mechanism but adds margin and position-limit constraints absent from offshore venues.
items.why: Described as the largest institutional-access expansion since the spot ETF approvals — it pulls perps trading onshore into a regulated framework.
